revision.text: #redirect[[en:Tsunugui_and_Ikugui]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

[[Image:Creation myths of Japan.svg|thumb|right|200px|天地開闢 [[media:Creation myths of Japan.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]] '''ツヌグイ'''・'''イクグイ'''は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]である。
ツヌグイ（ツヌグヒ）は[[古事記]]では'''角杙神'''、[[日本書紀]]では'''角樴尊'''と表記される。イクグイ（イクグヒ）は古事記では'''活杙神'''、日本書紀では'''活樴尊'''と表記される。
[[神世七代]]の第4代の神で、ツヌグイが男神、イクグイが女神である。「クイ（クヒ）」は「芽ぐむ」などの「クム」で、「角ぐむ」は角のように芽が出はじめる意、「活ぐむ」は生育しはじめるの意である。泥土が段々固まってきたことにより、生物が発成し育つことができるようになったことを示す神名である。
[[Category:日本の神|つぬくいいくくい]] {{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
[[Image:Creation myths of Japan.svg|thumb|right|200px|天地開闢 [[media:Creation myths of Japan.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]]{{神道}} '''ツヌグイ'''・'''イクグイ'''は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]である。
[[神世七代]]の第4代の神で、ツヌグイが男神、イクグイが女神である。「クイ（クヒ）」は「芽ぐむ」などの「クム」で、「角ぐむ」は角のように芽が出はじめる意、「活ぐむ」は生育しはじめるの意である。泥土が段々固まってきたことにより、生物が発成し育つことができるようになったことを示す神名である。
== 名称 ==

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 角杙神 | 世代名 = [[神世七代]] 第四代 | 先代= [[宇比地邇神]] | 次代= [[意富斗能地神]] | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[天津神]] | 全名 = 角杙神（ツヌグイノカミ） | 別名 = 角樴尊 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの --> | 陵所 = | 父 = | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 宮 = | 神社 = {{Plainlist| * [[物部神社]] * [[宮浦宮]] 等 }} | 記紀等 = [[古事記]]、[[先代旧事本紀]] }} {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 活杙神 | 世代名 = [[神世七代]] 第四代 | 先代= [[須比智邇神]] | 次代= [[大斗乃弁神]] | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[天津神]] | 全名 = 活杙神（イクグイノカミ） | 別名 = 活樴尊 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの --> | 陵所 = | 父 = | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 宮 = | 神社 = {{Plainlist| * [[物部神社]] * [[宮浦宮]] 等 }} | 記紀等 = [[古事記]]、[[先代旧事本紀]] }} [[Image:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|天地開闢 [[メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]] '''ツヌグイ'''（'''ツノグイ'''とも、{{smaller|[[歴史的仮名遣]]：}}'''ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ'''、角杙、角樴）・'''イクグイ'''（{{smaller|[[歴史的仮名遣]]：}}'''イクグヒ'''、活杙、活樴）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]である。 == 概要 == 『古事記』では兄を'''角杙神'''、妹を'''活杙神'''、『先代旧事本紀』では兄を'''角樴尊'''、妹を'''活樴尊'''と表記する。
『古事記』において[[神世七代]]の第四代の神とされ、兄角杙神が男神、妹活杙神が女神である。『[[日本書紀]]』では神世七代に数えられていない。
「クイ（クヒ）」は「芽ぐむ」などの「クム」で、「角ぐむ」は角のように芽が出はじめる意、「活ぐむ」は生育しはじめるの意とする説があり、泥土が段々固まってきたことにより、生物が発成し育つことができるようになったことを示す神名であるされる。
また名義は「角状の棒杙」と「活きいきとした棒杙」と考えられる{{Sfn|西宮|2014|p=325}}。 == 名称 == {|border="1" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="10" |-bgcolor="#EEEEEE" !書名!!1組!!2組!!3組!!4組!!5組!!6組!!7組!!8組!!9組 |- |[[古事記]]男神||宇比地邇神||'''角杙神'''||意富斗能地神||淤母陀流神||伊邪那岐神|||||||| |- |古事記妹||須比智邇神||'''活杙神'''||大斗乃辧神||阿夜訶志古泥神||伊邪那美神||||||||| |- |[[日本書紀]]1書第1（男神）||泥土煮尊||'''角樴尊'''||面足尊||伊奘諾尊||||||||||| |- |日本書紀1書第1（妹）||沙土煮尊||'''活樴尊'''||惶足尊||伊奘冉尊||||||||||| |- |[[先代旧事本紀]]（男神）||'''角樴尊'''（'''角龍魂尊'''）||泥土煮尊（泥土根尊）||大苫彦尊（大戸之道・大富道・大戸摩彦）||青橿城根尊（沫蕩尊・面足尊||伊弉諾尊||||||||| |- |先代旧事本紀妹||'''活樴尊'''||沙土煮尊（沙土根尊）||大苫邊尊（大戸之邊・大富邊・大戸摩姫）||吾屋橿城根尊（惶根尊・蚊鴈姫尊）||伊弉冉尊|||||||| |- |先代旧事本紀||天三降尊||天合尊（天鏡尊）||天八百日尊||天八十萬魂尊||高皇產靈尊（亦名、高魂尊・高木命）|||||||| |- |[[上記]]（男神）||うイぢにのみこと||'''つぬぐイのみこと'''||おおとのぢのみこと||おもだるのみこと||おおとぢのみこと||あをかしきねのみこと||あまのかかみのみこと||あわなぎのみこと||いざなぎのみこと |- |上記いも||すイぢにのみこと||'''いくぐイのみこと'''||おおとのべのみこと||あやかしこねのみこと||おおとべのみこと||あゆかしきねのみこと||あめのよろづのみこと||あわなみのみこと||いざなみのみこと |- |[[宋史]]日本傳男神||'''角龔魂尊'''||汲津丹尊||面垂見尊||國常立尊||天鑑尊||天萬尊||沫名杵尊||伊弉諾尊 |} == 祀る神社 == * [[物部神社]] 境内 神代七代社（[[島根県]][[大田市]][[川合町 (大田市)|川合町]]） * [[宮浦宮]]（[[鹿児島県]][[霧島市]][[福山町]]） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * <!-- にしみや -->{{Cite book|和書 |title=古事記 |volume= |date=2014年10月30日 |publisher=[[新潮社]] |ref={{SfnRef|西宮|2014}} |author=[[西宮一民]] 校注 |isbn=978-4-10-620801-0 |series=[[新潮日本古典集成]]（新装版）}} == 関連項目 == * [[別天津神]] * [[天津神]] * [[天地開闢]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/tsunoguinokami/ 角杙神 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/ikuguinokami/ 活杙神 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] {{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つぬくいいくくい}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:天津神]] [[Category:創造神]] [[Category:大地神]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 角杙神 | 世代名 = [[神世七代]] 第四代 | 先代= [[宇比地邇神]] | 次代= [[意富斗能地神]] | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[天津神]] | 全名 = 角杙神（ツヌグイノカミ） | 別名 = 角樴尊 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの --> | 陵所 = | 父 = | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 宮 = | 神社 = {{Plainlist| * [[物部神社]] * [[宮浦宮]] 等 }} | 記紀等 = [[古事記]]、[[先代旧事本紀]] }} {{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 活杙神 | 世代名 = [[神世七代]] 第四代 | 先代= [[須比智邇神]] | 次代= [[大斗乃弁神]] | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[天津神]] | 全名 = 活杙神（イクグイノカミ） | 別名 = 活樴尊 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの --> | 陵所 = | 父 = | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 宮 = | 神社 = {{Plainlist| * [[物部神社]] * [[宮浦宮]] 等 }} | 記紀等 = [[古事記]]、[[先代旧事本紀]] }} [[Image:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|天地開闢 [[メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]] '''ツヌグイ'''（'''ツノグイ'''とも、{{smaller|[[歴史的仮名遣]]：}}'''ツヌグヒ'''／'''ツノグヒ'''、角杙、角樴）・'''イクグイ'''（{{smaller|[[歴史的仮名遣]]：}}'''イクグヒ'''、活杙、活樴）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]である。 == 概要 == 『古事記』では兄を'''角杙神'''、妹を'''活杙神'''、『先代旧事本紀』では兄を'''角樴尊'''、妹を'''活樴尊'''と表記する。
『古事記』において[[神世七代]]の第四代の神とされ、兄角杙神が男神、妹活杙神が女神である。『[[日本書紀]]』では神世七代に数えられていない。
「クイ（クヒ）」は「芽ぐむ」などの「クム」で、「角ぐむ」は角のように芽が出はじめる意、「活ぐむ」は生育しはじめるの意とする説があり、泥土が段々固まってきたことにより、生物が発成し育つことができるようになったことを示す神名であるされる。
また名義は「角状の棒杙」と「活きいきとした棒杙」と考えられる{{Sfn|西宮|2014|p=325}}。 == 名称 == {|border="1" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="10" |-bgcolor="#EEEEEE" !書名!!1組!!2組!!3組!!4組!!5組!!6組!!7組!!8組!!9組 |- |[[古事記]]男神||宇比地邇神||'''角杙神'''||意富斗能地神||淤母陀流神||伊邪那岐神|||||||| |- |古事記妹||須比智邇神||'''活杙神'''||大斗乃辧神||阿夜訶志古泥神||伊邪那美神||||||||| |- |[[日本書紀]]1書第1（男神）||泥土煮尊||'''角樴尊'''||面足尊||伊奘諾尊||||||||||| |- |日本書紀1書第1（妹）||沙土煮尊||'''活樴尊'''||惶足尊||伊奘冉尊||||||||||| |- |[[先代旧事本紀]]（男神）||'''角樴尊'''（'''角龍魂尊'''）||泥土煮尊（泥土根尊）||大苫彦尊（大戸之道・大富道・大戸摩彦）||青橿城根尊（沫蕩尊・面足尊||伊弉諾尊||||||||| |- |先代旧事本紀妹||'''活樴尊'''||沙土煮尊（沙土根尊）||大苫邊尊（大戸之邊・大富邊・大戸摩姫）||吾屋橿城根尊（惶根尊・蚊鴈姫尊）||伊弉冉尊|||||||| |- |先代旧事本紀||天三降尊||天合尊（天鏡尊）||天八百日尊||天八十萬魂尊||高皇產靈尊（亦名、高魂尊・高木命）|||||||| |- |[[上記]]（男神）||うイぢにのみこと||'''つぬぐイのみこと'''||おおとのぢのみこと||おもだるのみこと||おおとぢのみこと||あをかしきねのみこと||あまのかかみのみこと||あわなぎのみこと||いざなぎのみこと |- |上記いも||すイぢにのみこと||'''いくぐイのみこと'''||おおとのべのみこと||あやかしこねのみこと||おおとべのみこと||あゆかしきねのみこと||あめのよろづのみこと||あわなみのみこと||いざなみのみこと |- |[[宋史]]日本傳男神||'''角龔魂尊'''||汲津丹尊||面垂見尊||國常立尊||天鑑尊||天萬尊||沫名杵尊||伊弉諾尊 |} == 祀る神社 == * [[物部神社]] 境内 神代七代社（[[島根県]][[大田市]][[川合町 (大田市)|川合町]]） * [[宮浦宮]]（[[鹿児島県]][[霧島市]][[福山町]]） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * <!-- にしみや -->{{Cite book|和書 |title=古事記 |volume= |date=2014年10月30日 |publisher=[[新潮社]] |ref={{SfnRef|西宮|2014}} |author=[[西宮一民]] 校注 |isbn=978-4-10-620801-0 |series=[[新潮日本古典集成]]（新装版）}} == 関連項目 == * [[別天津神]] * [[天津神]] * [[天地開闢]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/tsunoguinokami/ 角杙神 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/ikuguinokami/ 活杙神 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] {{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つぬくいいくくい}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:天津神]] [[Category:創造神]] [[Category:大地神]] {{shinto-stub}}

『古事記』において[[神世七代]]の第四代の神とされ、兄角杙神が男神、妹活杙神が女神である。『[[日本書紀]]』では神世七代に数えられていない。
「クイ（クヒ）」は「芽ぐむ」などの「クム」で、「角ぐむ」は角のように芽が出はじめる意、「活ぐむ」は生育しはじめるの意とする説があり、泥土が段々固まってきたことにより、生物が発成し育つことができるようになったことを示す神名であるされる。
また名義は「角状の棒杙」と「活きいきとした棒杙」と考えられる{{Sfn|西宮|2014|p=325}}。 == 名称 == {|border="1" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="10" |-bgcolor="#EEEEEE" !書名!!1組!!2組!!3組!!4組!!5組!!6組!!7組!!8組!!9組 |- |[[古事記]]男神||宇比地邇神||'''角杙神'''||意富斗能地神||淤母陀流神||伊邪那岐神|||||||| |- |古事記妹||須比智邇神||'''活杙神'''||大斗乃辧神||阿夜訶志古泥神||伊邪那美神||||||||| |- |[[日本書紀]]1書第1（男神）||泥土煮尊||'''角樴尊'''||面足尊||伊奘諾尊||||||||||| |- |日本書紀1書第1（妹）||沙土煮尊||'''活樴尊'''||惶足尊||伊奘冉尊||||||||||| |- |[[先代旧事本紀]]（男神）||'''角樴尊'''（'''角龍魂尊'''）||泥土煮尊（泥土根尊）||大苫彦尊（大戸之道・大富道・大戸摩彦）||青橿城根尊（沫蕩尊・面足尊||伊弉諾尊||||||||| |- |先代旧事本紀妹||'''活樴尊'''||沙土煮尊（沙土根尊）||大苫邊尊（大戸之邊・大富邊・大戸摩姫）||吾屋橿城根尊（惶根尊・蚊鴈姫尊）||伊弉冉尊|||||||| |- |先代旧事本紀||天三降尊||天合尊（天鏡尊）||天八百日尊||天八十萬魂尊||高皇產靈尊（亦名、高魂尊・高木命）|||||||| |- |[[上記]]（男神）||うイぢにのみこと||'''つぬぐイのみこと'''||おおとのぢのみこと||おもだるのみこと||おおとぢのみこと||あをかしきねのみこと||あまのかかみのみこと||あわなぎのみこと||いざなぎのみこと |- |上記いも||すイぢにのみこと||'''いくぐイのみこと'''||おおとのべのみこと||あやかしこねのみこと||おおとべのみこと||あゆかしきねのみこと||あめのよろづのみこと||あわなみのみこと||いざなみのみこと |- |[[宋史]]日本傳男神||'''角龔魂尊'''||汲津丹尊||面垂見尊||國常立尊||天鑑尊||天萬尊||沫名杵尊||伊弉諾尊 |} == 祀る神社 == * [[物部神社]] 境内 神代七代社（[[島根県]][[大田市]][[川合町 (大田市)|川合町]]） * [[宮浦宮]]（[[鹿児島県]][[霧島市]][[福山町]]） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * <!-- にしみや -->{{Cite book|和書 |title=古事記 |volume= |date=2014年10月30日 |publisher=[[新潮社]] |ref={{SfnRef|西宮|2014}} |author=[[西宮一民]] 校注 |isbn=978-4-10-620801-0 |series=[[新潮日本古典集成]]（新装版）}} == 関連項目 == * [[別天津神]] * [[天津神]] * [[天地開闢]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/tsunoguinokami/ 角杙神 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/ikuguinokami/ 活杙神 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] {{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つぬくいいくくい}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:天津神]] [[Category:創造神]] [[Category:大地神]] {{shinto-stub}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]], they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]], with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the [[:en:Kamiyonanayo|Kamiyonanayo]] in [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}.

== Overview ==
In {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}}, they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo}}, with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo}} in {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}.

== Overview ==
In {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}}, they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo}}, with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo}} in {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}.

== Overview ==
In {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi}}, they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo}}, with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo}} in {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|thumb|right|200px|The primordial deities of Japan {{ill|View as SVG|ja|メディア:Japanese Primordial Deities-ja.svg|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/View as SVG|qq=}}]]

'''Tsunugui''' (ツヌグイ or ツノグイ, {{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} ツヌグヒ／ツノグヒ, 角杙, 角樴) and '''Ikugui''' ({{smaller|Historical kana usage:}} イクグヒ, 活杙, 活樴) are deities that appear in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.

== Overview ==
In {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, Tsunugui-no-Kami is described as the elder brother, and Ikugui-no-Kami as the younger sister. In {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}, they are referred to as Tsunugui-no-Mikoto (角樴尊) and Ikugui-no-Mikoto (活樴尊), respectively.

The deities are part of the fourth generation in the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}}, with Tsunugui-no-Kami depicted as a male deity and Ikugui-no-Kami as a female deity in Kojiki. However, they are not listed among the {{ill|Kamiyonanayo|en|Kamiyonanayo|WD=Q3275893|lt=Kamiyonanayo|1=Kamiyonanayo}} in {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}.

The term "Kui" (or "Kuh") is thought to derive from "Kumu" (to sprout), implying that "Tsunugui" signifies the beginning of sprouting like horns, and "Ikugui" suggests the early stages of growth and vitality. These names metaphorically represent the process of solidification of mud and soil, marking the conditions necessary for life to emerge.

Their names are also interpreted as "a horn-like stake" (角杙) and "a vibrant stake" (活杙).{{Sfn|Nishimiya|2014|p=325}}
